turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: The word for how in Spanish is cómo.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Cómo. How do you think you would say, how are you?, talking informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Cómo estás? Because when you ask somebody how they are, you're referring to their state. ¿ Cómo estás? And formally, how would you ask?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And if you're asking more than one person?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very nice. The word for where is dónde. So how would you say, where are you?, talking informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Dónde estás? I don't know where we are. So what was I don't know?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No sé. Good. I don't know where we are:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. No sé donde estamos. Good. How would you say, I am here?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Estoy aquí, Estoy acá. How would you say, I want to be here?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Quiero estar aquí. Good. How would you say, I'm going to be here later? What was the word for late?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tarde. Good. And later or more late?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So I'm going to be here later:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Voy a estar aquí más tarde

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Voy a estar aquí más tarde. Voy is I go or I'm going. How do you say you go or you are going, speaking informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Vas. Good. So how would you say, where are you going to be? So break it up: where are you going to be?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Yes. ¿ Dónde vas a estar? W here are you going to be? Good. So let's run through the forms again, and let's use the words for emphasis just to practice them. So I am is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Yo estoy. He is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Él está. She is:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And you (talking formally) are:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Usted está. Good. They are, using the word for they:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Ellos están. Or for feminine:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. What is we are?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And for feminine:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So nosotros changes to nosotras. You are, talking informally:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And if we used the word for you:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Tú estás. Tú estás. Good. How would you ask more than one person, where do you have to be later? So the first bit is where.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Do you have?, and we are talking to you (plural), which is the same as they. Do you have? Yeah, what's to have?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Tener. Good. And we have it as - tain in words like maintain - mantener, contain - contener, sustain - sostener, entertain - entretener, obtain - obtener. That will help us. So first we want to build you guys have from tener.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So where do you have to be later, to you (plural). Try it.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Slower., and I’ve forgotten the other one now.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: It's okay, work it out again.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: There is something we need to add to tienen to get this meaning of have to, and it's not a comfortable translation.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Later:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Dónde tienen que estar más tarde? Good. You might want to say, he must be here, and you can use this in the same way as you do in English, to refer to probability. Oh, he must be here by now, or something like this. You have the same use in Spanish. And must is the same as to owe, which is related to debt. So what was to owe?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Deber. Good. So he owes or he must:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Debe. This e doesn't split. Good. Debe. So he must be here:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And he must be tired:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Debe estar cansado. They must be tired:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Very good. What if they were females?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: All together. Don't miss the fun.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Deben estar cansadas. To remain was quedar. Quedar.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: And to get the meaning of I stay, we say, I remain myself. So how would that sound? I remain myself:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Me quedo. I am staying. How would we are staying, or we remain ourselves, be?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. We are not going to stay, because we are very tired. We are not going to stay, because we are very tired. So the first bit that needs to command all of our attention is we are not going:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: No vamos. We are not going to stay, so we are not going to remain ourselves:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. No vamos a quedarnos or no nos vamos a quedar. You can have this as well. No nos vamos a quedar. Because, same as why:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Porque. We are very tired. And let's be a masculine or a mixed group. We are very tired:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, now all together and not from memory. Thinking about it again, block by block. If you rush it, you will trip and tripping is very time-consuming. So, we are not going to remain ourselves. To stay:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Because we are very tired.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Well done. No vamos a quedarnos, no nos vamos a quedar porque, estamos muy cansados. Very good.
